entries.lede: Full-wave high-vacuum rectifier with a 4-pin UX base. Used in 1930s–40s valve receivers and amplifiers where a high-current B+ supply was needed. Electrically identical to the more familiar octal-based 5U4G, just on the older 4-pin socket.
entries.country: USA (also made by Marconi CA, Brimar UK, others under licence)
entries.heating: Directly heated filament, 5.0 V AC, 3.0 A
entries.applications_prose: HT power supply rectifier in valve radios (broadcast and communications receivers, 1933–50s), Hammond-style organs, early hi-fi and guitar amplifiers, and laboratory power supplies needing a high-current 5V-heated rectifier on the older 4-pin socket.
entries.value_prose: Used / tested pulls trade lower (AUD 10–25). RCA black-plate and JAN-CRC military variants command a premium. Less sought-after than the 5U4G because most modern amps use the octal base.

entries.lede: Pre-octal full-wave high-vacuum rectifier — one of the original Western Electric / RCA tube families, dating to 1927. 5-volt, 2-amp directly-heated filament on the 4-pin UX socket. The octal-era replacement is the 5Y3G, with which the 80 is electrically identical.
entries.heating: Directly heated filament, 5.0 V AC, 2.0 A
entries.application_short: HT (B+) supply in domestic receivers & small amps
entries.applications_prose: The 80 was the standard B+ rectifier in AC-mains broadcast receivers from the late 1920s through the 1940s — most Radiola, Astor and AWA console sets of the era used one. Also common in Hammond-style organs and laboratory power supplies that needed a modest-current rectifier on the older 4-pin socket.
entries.value_prose: Common Australian-made AWV / Radiotron stock is at the lower end; early RCA mesh-plate or balloon-shape (UX-280) variants trade higher. Used / tested-good pulls AUD 8–20.
sources: Aussie Audio Mart — vintage valve listings

entries.lede: Variable-mu (remote-cutoff) RF / IF pentode introduced by RCA in 1932. Effectively identical electricals to the better-known 6D6 — same heater, same 6-pin small base, same characteristics — and the two are routinely listed as direct equivalents.
entries.application_short: RF and IF amplification with AGC (variable-mu action)
entries.applications_prose: Standard variable-mu pentode in mid-1930s superhet broadcast and short-wave receivers — the RF and IF stages of almost every domestic radio from about 1933 to 1936, before the octal-base 6U7G and miniature 6BA6 took over. Pairs naturally with the sharp-cutoff 6C6 as detector / first-AF.
entries.value_prose: Plentiful in vintage-radio circles, so prices stay modest. Used / tested-good pulls AUD 5–12. Premium for sealed RCA black-plate or Marconi-Osram examples.

entries.lede: Variable-mu (remote-cutoff) RF / IF pentode — the direct successor to the type 78, with which it shares heater, base, pinout and characteristics. Approximately five boxes of this type are present in the source frame, all with handwritten labels on Australian AWV "Super Radiotron" cartons.
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code is handwritten on the carton sticker
entries.applications_prose: RF and IF stages of broadcast and short-wave superhet receivers from 1933 onward — the workhorse remote-cutoff pentode of the pre-octal era. Australian sets from AWA, Astor, Healing and HMV used it extensively. The variable-mu characteristic lets the stage gain be reduced cleanly by AGC bias.
entries.value_prose: Common and cheap on the second-hand market — supply outstrips demand because most surviving radios already have one. Used pulls AUD 5–10. Boxed Australian-made Radiotron examples in original cartons (as here) carry a small collector premium.

entries.lede: Sharp-cutoff RF / detector pentode — the fixed-mu counterpart to the 6D6. Same 6-pin small base, same 6.3 V heater, same envelope; the difference is the control-grid characteristic, which suits first-detector and AF roles where AGC isn't applied.
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code is handwritten on the carton sticker
entries.application_short: Detector, audio voltage amplifier, first-stage RF without AGC
entries.applications_prose: Where the 6D6 was the controlled-gain RF/IF tube, the 6C6 was the fixed-gain partner — used as the grid-leak or plate detector, the first audio amplifier stage, or as a low-noise RF stage in short-wave sets. Paired with a 6D6 in countless 1930s Australian superhets. Octal-base successor: 6J7.
entries.value_prose: Roughly the same market as the 6D6 — common, plentiful, modestly priced. Used pulls AUD 5–10. The 6C6 is slightly scarcer than the 6D6 since fewer were needed per set.

entries.lede: Indirectly-heated full-wave rectifier on the octal base — the small-signal partner to the 5Y3GT for receiver-sized B+ supplies. Heater is 6.3 V so it can share a transformer winding with the signal valves, simplifying AC/DC and car-radio designs.
entries.application_short: B+ supply for receivers, small amps, car radios
entries.applications_prose: The 6X5GT was the rectifier of choice for valve car radios and small AC mains receivers from the late 1930s onward, because the 6.3 V heater meant one transformer winding (or vibrator inverter) could supply every valve in the set. Also common in test gear, small organs and intercom amplifiers.
entries.value_prose: Common and widely available. Premium for early black-glass GT envelopes or Marconi-Osram "red label" examples. The closely-related 6X4 (B7G miniature) is the post-war replacement in compact sets.

entries.lede: The Marconi-Osram / GEC KT66 is one of the most sought-after British audio output valves ever made — a kinkless beam tetrode that became the voice of early Marshall guitar amps, the Leak TL12 and the McIntosh MC75. Roughly interchangeable with the American 6L6 family, but with a distinctly British tonal signature and premium build quality.
entries.country: United Kingdom (Marconi-Osram Valve Co. / GEC, Hammersmith)
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code handwritten in pencil on the GEC carton
entries.application_short: Class-A or AB push-pull audio output (~30 W per pair)
entries.mounting: Any orientation; base-down preferred for amps
entries.applications_prose: Originally designed by M-O Valve Co. as a higher-quality British answer to the RCA 6L6. Became famous as the output stage of the Leak Point One and TL/10 amplifiers, the McIntosh MC30 and MC75, and — most famously — early Marshall JTM45 guitar amplifiers before Marshall switched to the 5881 / 6L6GC for cost reasons. Studio engineers still pay a premium for genuine GEC KT66s for Marshall reissues and bespoke hi-fi builds.
entries.value_note: NOS, boxed, single tube · matched pairs higher
entries.value_prose: Genuine GEC / M-O Valve KT66s in original GEC cartons are the premium tier: AUD 300 / pair is common on Aussie Audio Mart and matched quads run AUD 500–700. Reissue Genalex (New Sensor) tubes sell for AUD 80–120 each new and don't trade on the vintage market. Authenticate by the GEC etch on the glass and the brown micanol base — fakes exist.
sources: Aussie Audio Mart — GEC KT66 NOS listings

entries.lede: Australian-made duo-diode pentode — two diodes for AM detection / AGC plus a sharp-cutoff pentode in one envelope, all in a single 7-pin small base with a top-cap control grid. The "S" suffix on Australian Radiotron / AWV cartons indicates a "Super" / Sydney-made variant of the otherwise identical 6B7.
entries.application_short: Combined detector / AVC / first-AF stage in 1930s superhets
entries.applications_prose: The 6B7 family did three jobs in one envelope — the two diode plates handled second-detection of the IF signal and produced AGC voltage, while the pentode acted as the first audio amplifier. Standard fit in Australian and US broadcast superhets from about 1933 until the octal 6B8G / 6SQ7 took over. Equivalent to the German EBF2 in function (though pinout differs).
entries.value_prose: Less common than the 6D6 / 6C6 but not rare. Boxed AWV Australian-made examples (as here) carry a small premium with vintage-radio restorers. Used pulls AUD 5–12.

entries.lede: Sharp-cutoff RF / IF / detector pentode on the octal base — the direct octal successor to the pre-octal 6C6. Top-cap control grid, 6.3 V indirectly-heated cathode, used as the first audio amp, plate detector or low-noise RF stage in 1937–50s receivers and signal generators.
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code is handwritten on the carton sticker
entries.envelope_detail: ST or GT glass, top-cap control grid
entries.base_detail: International octal with top-cap control grid
entries.application_short: Sharp-cutoff RF / IF / detector / first-AF without AGC
entries.applications_prose: Octal version of the 6C6 — fixed-gain RF pentode with low noise and a sharp cutoff, used as the first IF stage, as a plate-detector, or as a low-distortion audio voltage amplifier. Common in laboratory signal generators (e.g. BC-221 frequency meter) and in PA systems alongside the remote-cutoff 6K7.
entries.value_prose: Common and inexpensive. Premium for metal-envelope 6J7 (versus glass) and for JAN-CRC military variants. Used pulls AUD 5–12.

entries.lede: Octal triode-heptode frequency converter — combines a local oscillator triode and a heptode mixer in one envelope, the first-stage workhorse of late-1930s and 1940s superhet receivers. Australian-made by AWV under the Super Radiotron label.
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code is handwritten on the carton sticker
entries.application_short: First-stage mixer / oscillator in superhet receivers
entries.applications_prose: Standard frequency converter in Australian valve receivers from the late 1930s through the war years — produces the intermediate-frequency signal by mixing the incoming RF with the local oscillator's signal, both happening inside the same envelope. AWA, Astor, Healing and HMV sets all used it. The miniature post-war successor is the 6BE6 (B7G heptode, separate oscillator stage) or the all-in-one 6BE8.
entries.value_prose: Slightly less common than the 6D6 / 6C6 family because each set only used one. Used pulls AUD 8–15. Boxed Australian-made examples carry a modest collector premium.

entries.lede: The 6DB5 is a miniature noval beam power tube derived from the octal 6W6GT family — essentially a 6W6 with a reduced plate structure to fit a standard 9-pin envelope. Because of the smaller plate it cannot match the full plate dissipation of the 6W6GT, but it delivers useful audio output power from a compact package well-suited to post-war television and radio chassis.
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code identified by the model from the low-resolution source photo; crop is pixelated, worth physically confirming
entries.function: Beam power amplifier (vertical deflection / audio output)
entries.application_short: Vertical deflection amplifier and audio output stage in television and radio receivers.
entries.applications_prose: The 6DB5 was designed primarily as a vertical deflection amplifier in television receivers, where its compact noval envelope was preferred over older octal types. It was also used as a single-ended audio output valve in compact receiver designs operating from low-voltage B+ supplies around 130–180 V.
entries.value_prose: The 6DB5 is a common television-era type with modest collector appeal; NOS RCA examples in original boxes command a small premium over tested pulls. Demand is primarily from vintage TV restorers.

entries.lede: The 5BK7B (and its close relatives 5BK7 and 5BK7A) is a miniature medium-mu twin triode with a 4.7 V heater designed for use as a combined oscillator and mixer (frequency converter) stage in television receivers. The 'B' suffix denotes a later revision with tightened electrical specifications.
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code identified by the model from the low-resolution source photo; crop is pixelated, worth physically confirming
entries.application_short: Combined oscillator and mixer stage in VHF television tuners and receivers.
entries.applications_prose: The 5BK7B was used principally in the front-end tuner sections of television receivers as a local-oscillator / mixer (frequency converter) tube. Its 4.7 V heater fitted series-string heater chains common in transformerless TV sets of the era. The two triode sections could be used as a self-oscillating mixer, simplifying the tuner circuit.
entries.value_prose: The 5BK7B is a specialised television type with limited demand outside vintage TV restoration; RCA examples in original boxes are relatively scarce and attract modest premiums from collectors.

entries.lede: The 6CU5 is a 7-pin miniature beam power tube descended from the 50C5 / 50B5 family — the 6 V heater version sharing identical internal construction with the 12C5, 17C5, 25C5, and 50C5. It was used as an audio output valve in the output stage of television receivers and transformerless AC/DC radio sets.
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code identified by the model from the low-resolution source photo; crop is pixelated, worth physically confirming
entries.application_short: Audio output stage in television receivers and transformerless AC/DC radio sets.
entries.applications_prose: The 6CU5 was primarily employed as the audio output tube in the sound section of television receivers and compact AC/DC radios of the late 1950s and 1960s. Its 7-pin B7G base allowed compact chassis layout, and its low operating voltage made it ideal for transformerless designs with line-derived B+ of around 100–130 V.
entries.value_prose: The 6CU5 is a very common consumer TV/radio type with broad NOS availability from multiple manufacturers; RCA examples in original boxes are priced modestly and appeal mainly to vintage radio and TV restorers.

entries.lede: The 6AS8 is a miniature noval single-diode / sharp-cutoff pentode combination tube used in AM/FM radio and television intermediate-frequency circuits. The diode section serves as a detector or AVC rectifier while the sharp-cutoff pentode provides IF amplification, combining two functions in one 9-pin envelope.
entries.confidence_label: Medium · code identified by the model from the low-resolution source photo; crop is pixelated, worth physically confirming
entries.application_short: Combined IF amplifier and detector/AVC stage in AM/FM radio and television receivers.
entries.applications_prose: The 6AS8 was used in the intermediate-frequency strip of AM/FM radios and television sets, where its pentode section amplified the IF signal and its built-in diode provided signal detection and/or automatic volume control (AVC) rectification. Combining both functions in one envelope reduced component count and simplified circuit layout in compact receiver designs of the late 1950s.
entries.value_prose: The 6AS8 is a common consumer-grade IF/detector tube with modest collector interest; NOS RCA examples typically sell at the lower end of the vintage miniature tube price range, with original-box examples commanding a small premium.
sources: Frank's Electron Tube Data Sheets – 6AS8 PDF
